prediction: Showing interest in the accomplishments of others or having a reasonable amount of pride in ourselves is not wrong. We are not supposed to be ashamed of our family, culture, or ethnic background. On the other hand, there is a type of pride that can quietly and surely destroy our relationship with Jehovah and with our friends. Such pride might cause us to resent and resist wise counsel rather than humbly accept it. This type of pride is defined as “an excessive or inordinate self-esteem” or “a haughty attitude or behavior shown by those who believe that they are better than others.” Jehovah hates a haughty person. And Satan must love to see people become proud because they reflect his own personality. Imagine how pleased Satan must have been with the pride of Nabal, Pharaoh, and Absalom—all of whom displayed undue pride!—. w155/15 2:5, 6
reference: Feeling good about the works of others and even having a degree of pride in ourselves can be healthy. We are not expected to be ashamed of our family, culture, or the region where we grew up. On the other hand, there is a kind of pride that can eat away at relationships and damage our friendship with Jehovah. Such pride can cause us to resent and reject needed counsel rather than humbly accept it. This type of pride is defined as “inordinate self-esteem” or “a haughty attitude shown by people who believe, often unjustifiably, that they are better than others.” Jehovah hates arrogant pride. But Satan must delight to see humans display boastful pride, for it echoes his own arrogance. How Satan must have reveled in the boasting of such men as Nimrod, Pharaoh, and Absalom—all of whom fell victim to improper pride!—. w155/15 2:5, 6

prediction: What, though, if someone says or does something that upsets us? We can maintain our joy if we act in accord with David’s words:“Tremble and do not sin; let the sun not set with you in a provoked state.” After hearing thoughtless words or experiencing thoughtless actions, we should not sin by retaliating. We can express our feelings in prayer before we go to bed. When we pray about the matter, we may be able to put things in perspective and be moved to act in a forgiving way. The apostle Paul’s counsel is especially helpful in this regard, based on: “Be wrathful, and yet do not sin; let the sun not set with you in a provoked state; neither allow place for the Devil.”
reference: What if someone says or does things that upset us? We can remain joyful if we do what David says:“Be agitated, but do not sin. Have your say in your heart, upon your bed, and keep silent.” If we have been the object of unkind words or actions, let us not sin by retaliating. We can have our say in our heart while in bed. If we pray about the matter, we may see it in a different light and be moved to forgive out of love. Noteworthy in this regard is the apostle Paul’s counsel, apparently based on: “Be wrathful, and yet do not sin; let the sun not set with you in a provoked state, neither allow place for the Devil.”

But in reality they had a distorted view of the Law. They had come to stress the letter of the Law rather than its spirit. And they emphasized their traditions, putting such above the teaching of God. As a consequence, the people in general were not taught to think in terms of principles.

prediction: A proper regard for congregation elders is based on the good, wise counsel found in God’s Word. We can and should commend them for their hard work and effort and should strive to follow the direction they give. Doing so may help them to carry out their responsibilities with joy. However, we should not imitate an elder “famous” for his style of dress and grooming, his manner of public speaking, or his way of talking. If we did, we might be giving undue attention to him. Our model should be Christ.
reference: Rendering proper honor to congregation elders is, of course, to be guided by the perfect and wise principles found in God’s Word. We can and should commend them for their hard work and strive to cooperate with directions that they provide. Our doing that may help them to continue carrying out their duties with joy. Love one another intensely from the heart.—.

Jehovah sets the example for us. His love is so intense that if we are loyal to him, nothing can break that bond. The Greek word rendered “intensely” conveys the idea of stretching outward—even straining. At times, we may need to “stretch” and “strain” in order to have tender affection for a fellow believer. When others upset us, we need to continue “putting up with one another in love, earnestly endeavoring to maintain the oneness of the spirit in the uniting bond of peace.” We will look beyond our brothers’ faults and try our best to view our brothers as Jehovah does. It is not always easy to show tender affection for our brothers and sisters, especially when we are aware of their faults. prediction:
All those desiring to live with godly devotion in association with Christ Jesus will also be persecuted.—.

On the night before our Lord Jesus died, he said that all who choose to be his followers will be hated. Even now, faithful Witnesses of Jehovah have been persecuted by those who oppose true worship. As this system draws to its close, we can expect our enemies to oppose us even more. How can we prepare now to deal with persecution? We do not need to imagine every possible thing that could happen to us. If we did, we might be overwhelmed by fear and anxiety. We could allow the threats we imagine to make us surrender before any real test comes. Fear is a powerful weapon that our “adversary, the Devil,” tries to use against us. It is vital that we strengthen our relationship with Jehovah and that we do so now. w19.072 ¶1-3

All those desiring to live with godly devotion in association with Christ Jesus will also be persecuted.—.

Up until now, faithful Christian Witnesses of Jehovah have been persecuted by those who oppose true worship. As the end of this system of things draws closer, we expect our enemies to oppose us even more. How can we prepare ourselves now to face persecution? We do not need to imagine all the things that could happen to us. If we did, we could be overwhelmed with fear and anxiety. prediction: “The Lamb” opens the third seal, and “a black horse” appears. “And the one seated on it had a balance scale in his hand.” Why, there is someone riding with the rider of the war horse! It is the rider of the famine horse. In both world wars, food shortages plagued many countries. Food rationing, symbolized by “a balance scale,” became the norm in warring nations. And the greatest famine of all time came at the end of World War I. The Nationof June 7, 1919, reported that 32,000,000 people in India were “approaching death by starvation.” World’s Workof March 1921 said that in one province of China 15,000 people were dying every day of starvation. The New YorkTimesbook review in October 1921 quoted a British report stating that “more than 35,000,000 [in Russia] are threatened with famine and disease.” Similarly, food shortages followed World War II, as the magazineLookreported on June 11, 1946: “One fourth of the world is starving today.”
reference: As the “Lamb” opens the third seal, “a black horse” charges forth. “And the one seated upon it had a pair of scales in his hand.” Ah, here is a companion rider to the horseman of total warfare! This is the horseman that brings famine. During both the world wars, famine conditions plagued many countries. Food rationing, as represented by the “pair of scales,” became the norm for the citizens of the warring nations. And in the wake of World War I came the greatest famine of all history. The Nationof June 7, 1919, reported that 32,000,000 people in India were “on the verge of starvation.”World’s Workof March 1921 stated that in northern China alone 15,000 people were dying every day from hunger. The New YorkTimespublicationCurrent History Magazineof October 1921 quoted a British report that in Russia “no fewer than 35,000,000 people are stalked by the grim spectre of famine and pestilence.” Similar famine conditions prevailed following World War II, whenLookmagazine of June 11, 1946, reported: “A fourth of the world is starving today.”

prediction: Today, many expectant mothers spend months preparing for the arrival of their new baby, and Mary was likely no different. But this was to be her first. Unexpected events, however, made her preparations more difficult. Caesar Augustus had ordered a census and registration in the cities of origin of each person. So Joseph took Mary, who was likely close to full term, and traveled some 90 miles (150 km), perhaps on a donkey! Bethlehem was crowded, and Mary needed a private place to give birth, but the only space available was in a stable. For Mary, giving birth in a stable must have been a challenge. She may have felt embarrassed and even frightened.
reference: Today, many mothers-to-be spend months preparing for the arrival of a baby, and Mary may have done the same. This was to be her first child. Yet, unexpected events complicated her plans. Caesar Augustus decreed a census, requiring all to register in their town of origin. So Joseph took Mary, now in her ninth month of pregnancy, on a journey of about 90 miles[150 km], likely on a donkey’s back! Bethlehem was crowded and Mary needed somewhere private to give birth, but the only place available was a stable. Giving birth in a stable must have been hard for Mary. She may well have been both embarrassed and scared.

prediction: Another way to cultivate patience is to consider how we view the world and our personal circumstances. Try to see things as Jehovah does. For example, consider how he views human suffering. He has felt pain because of human suffering; yet, he has not been overcome by grief, unable to do anything. He has sent his only-begotten Son “to break up the works of the Devil” and undo all the damage that Satan has done to mankind. In fact, it will be proved that suffering is temporary but God’s solution is eternal. Similarly, rather than allow the present wickedness of Satan’s system to cause us to lose patience or to become impatient with the timing of its end, let us exercise faith in the things unseen that will last forever. Jehovah has set a special limit for wickedness, and he will act at the right time.
reference: Another way to develop patience is by considering how we should view the world and our personal circumstances. Try to look at matters as Jehovah does. For example, think about how he views human suffering. He has long been pained when humans suffer; yet, he has not been overcome with grief, incapable of doing good. He sent his only-begottenSon “to break up the works of the Devil” and undo all the damage Satan has brought upon humans. In reality, the suffering will prove to have been temporary, but God’s solution is eternal. Similarly, instead of letting ourselves become overwhelmed by the present wickedness of Satan’s rule or impatient about when it will end, let us put faith in the unseen things that will last forever. Jehovah has set a specific limit for wickedness, and he will act right on time.

prediction: Unlike humans, God always uses his power for good. “Jehovah is watching the earth continually; he sustains those loyal to him with power.” Jehovah exercises his power with restraint. In patience, God withholds punishment from the wicked so that they may have time to repent. In mercy, he causes the sun to shine on both good and bad people. reference: Unlike many humans, God always uses his power for good. “As regards Jehovah, his eyes are roving about through all the earth to show his strength in behalf of those whose heart is complete toward him.” Jehovah directs his power in a controlled way. Patience holds back God’s execution of the wicked to give them the opportunity to repent. Love moves him to make the sun shine upon all kinds of men—righteous and unrighteous. prediction: What could hinder us from showing patience? A tendency to be impatient. What does that mean? Well, a person who lacks confidence that the end is near might start to make some changes in his routine, just in case Jehovah’s word does not come true. In other words, he might think, ‘I will wait and see whether Jehovah is right about what he has said.’ He may try to make a name for himself in this world, seek financial security instead of putting Kingdom interests first, or believe in higher education to ensure a comfortable life now. Are those not signs of a lack of faith? Remember, Paul encouraged us to imitate the faithful ones who received promises from Jehovah “through faith and patience.” Jehovah is determined to bring this wicked world to an end, and he will not be late. Until then, let us not be distracted from our service to Jehovah. Instead, let us keep on the watch and keep busy preaching the good news, which brings complete satisfaction right now.
reference: Something that interferes with our manifesting godly patience is a wait-and-see attitude. What does that mean? Well, a person who lacks confidence that the end is near might start to make alternate arrangements, so to speak, in case things do not work out as Jehovah said they would. In other words, he might be thinking, ‘I will wait and see if Jehovah really is true to his word.’ He might then try to make a name for himself in this world, to seek financial security instead of putting God’s Kingdom first, or to trust in higher education to secure a comfortable life now. Really, though, would that not be evidence of a lack of faith? Remember that Paul urged us to become imitators of those faithful ones who received promises from Jehovah “through faith and patience.” Jehovah will not allow this wicked system to exist any longer than is absolutely necessary according to his purpose. In the interim, we must avoid just going through the motions of serving Jehovah. Rather, we need to remain alert and work diligently at preaching the good news, which is a source of incomparable satisfaction even now.

How much more so that is true of maintaining our spiritual health and strength!

Bad associations spoil useful habits.—.

To avoid spoiling our useful habits, we must not have as close associates those who practice bad things. This applies not only to associating with unbelieving wrongdoers but also to associating with those who claim to worship Jehovah but who deliberately violate his laws. If such professing Christians engage in serious wrongdoing and do not repent, we do not continue to associate with them. If we were to associate with those who do not obey God’s laws, we might have the tendency to do what they do in order to be accepted by them.
